_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against Hirst 2008 systematic review (Surv Ophthalmol), AAO Preferred Practice Pattern (Cornea/External Disease), Kenyon 1985 conjunctival autograft landmark paper, and Tan 1997 pterygium morphology grading. Astigmatism numbers checked against Lin & Stern (Cornea 1998) and Maheshwari topographic studies.

h1Title: Central Pterygium of the Eye: Symptoms, Surgery, and Recovery
heroOverview: Central pterygium is the advanced stage of pterygium in which the wing-shaped fibrovascular growth has crossed the limbus and encroached on the pupillary axis of the cornea. Once the leading edge approaches within 3 mm of the visual center, it distorts the corneal curvature, induces 1 to 7 diopters of with-the-rule astigmatism, drops best-corrected visual acuity, and can produce monocular diplopia. Unlike peripheral pterygium, which is monitored, central pterygium is a surgical lesion: conjunctival autograft excision is indicated even in otherwise quiet eyes to halt encroachment, restore corneal optics, and prevent permanent scarring of the visual axis. Modern autograft techniques achieve recurrence rates of 5 to 10 percent, compared with 30 to 90 percent for older bare-sclera removal.

keyStats.prevalence: Pterygium affects 10.2% of adults globally (Liu 2013 meta-analysis); central (grade III-IV) subset is roughly 5-15% of pterygium cases
keyStats.demographics: Men 2-3x more affected than women in equatorial latitudes; peak age 30-60
keyStats.avgAge: Most central pterygia diagnosed between ages 40 and 65 after years of UV exposure
keyStats.globalCases: Highest prevalence in the 'pterygium belt' within 37 degrees latitude of the equator; rates exceed 20% in some Australian, Caribbean, and sub-Saharan cohorts

definition: Central pterygium (ICD-10: H11.04) is a wing-shaped fibrovascular conjunctival growth that has extended across the corneal limbus and reached or crossed the edge of the pupillary axis, classified as Tan grade T3 (fleshy) or Maheshwari grade III-IV. The lesion originates at the interpalpebral conjunctiva, typically nasally, and advances onto the cornea as a triangular pannus of elastotic stromal tissue, fibroblasts, neovascularization, and inflammatory cells. Once the head of the pterygium reaches the central 4 mm optical zone, it mechanically flattens the underlying cornea along the meridian of advance, producing characteristic with-the-rule astigmatism that compounds rapidly with the last 1 to 2 mm of advancement. Refractive penalty rises steeply: pterygia within 3 mm of the visual axis routinely induce 1 to 7 diopters of cylindrical error, irregular astigmatism on topography, and a measurable loss of best-corrected visual acuity. The condition is functionally distinct from peripheral pterygium (asymptomatic, observed only) and from pseudopterygium (post-inflammatory conjunctival adhesion without limbal stem-cell involvement). It is managed by cornea-trained ophthalmologists, with co-management by optometry during visual rehabilitation. Untreated central pterygium causes permanent stromal scarring and amblyogenic refractive error in adults.

typesClassification.description: Episcleral vessels under the body of the pterygium are clearly visible. Slower growth but can still encroach centrally; recurrence after surgery is lowest in this morphology.
Episcleral vessels are partially obscured. Most common presentation in clinic; behaviour is intermediate between atrophic and fleshy forms.
Episcleral vessels are completely obscured by thick fibrovascular tissue. Highest recurrence rate (up to 24% even with autograft) and most likely to encroach rapidly on the visual axis.

typesClassification.type: Maheshwari grade III (encroaching) and IV (crossing) pterygium
typesClassification.description: Grade III lesions have crossed the limbus and reach the pupillary margin; grade IV lesions cross the pupil center. Both are surgical indications regardless of inflammation.
Nasal and temporal heads converge on the cornea simultaneously. Less common but typically requires bilateral autograft or amniotic membrane reconstruction.

primarySymptoms: Gradual decrease in vision in the affected eye, often described as blurring that does not fully correct with new glasses because the underlying problem is induced astigmatism rather than simple refractive change.
Monocular diplopia or ghosting of images, caused by the irregular astigmatism from the pterygium edge distorting the cornea over the pupillary axis.
Persistent foreign body sensation, gritty feel, or the feeling of something in the eye, particularly when blinking, due to mechanical lid friction over the elevated pterygium head.
Visible pink or red triangular tissue extending from the inner corner of the eye onto the cornea, often noticed by family or in photographs before the patient registers it.
Redness and visible engorged vessels over the pterygium body, particularly after sun, wind, or dust exposure; flares can last days.
Dry-eye symptoms including burning, reflex tearing, and end-of-day discomfort, because the pterygium disrupts the precorneal tear film over its surface.
Cosmetic concern about the appearance of the eye, especially when the lesion is large enough to be visible at conversational distance.
Difficulty wearing contact lenses, as the raised pterygium prevents proper lens centration and causes lens edge irritation.
Glare and halos around lights at night, particularly when the pupil dilates over a partly covered visual axis.
Headache and asthenopia from prolonged ciliary effort to compensate for fluctuating astigmatism during the day.
earlyWarningSigns: A previously peripheral pterygium that has crossed the limbus and reached within 3 mm of the pupillary center on slit-lamp examination
An increase of more than 1 diopter of cylindrical refractive error on serial spectacle prescriptions over 6 to 12 months
Visible flattening of the central cornea on topography along the meridian of pterygium advance
New onset of monocular ghosting or diplopia in an eye known to have pterygium
Best-corrected visual acuity dropping by one line or more on serial visits without other ocular pathology
emergencySigns: Sudden severe pain, photophobia, and decreased vision with a known pterygium, which can indicate corneal ulceration or perforation under the lesion
Acute purulent discharge with rapid enlargement of the pterygium body, suggesting superimposed bacterial conjunctivitis or dacryocystitis
Sudden onset of double vision in both eyes or with the other eye covered that persists, which warrants same-day ophthalmology assessment
Rapid pterygium growth over weeks rather than months, especially in an immunosuppressed patient, which can mask ocular surface squamous neoplasia

causes.description: Cumulative UV-B exposure damages limbal stem cells, allowing aberrant conjunctival migration onto the cornea. UV is the strongest single environmental driver and explains the equatorial distribution of pterygium.

Mechanical irritation and tear-film instability from sand, dust, smoke, and low humidity accelerate fibroblast activation in the limbal stroma, promoting pterygium growth and pushing peripheral lesions toward the visual axis.

The limbus normally serves as a barrier preventing conjunctival epithelium from crossing onto the cornea. Localized stem-cell deficiency (UV-induced, inflammatory, or genetic) lets the conjunctival tissue migrate centrally.
First-degree relatives of pterygium patients have roughly twice the risk. Polymorphisms in TGF-beta and matrix metalloproteinase genes are associated with aggressive, centrally encroaching lesions.
Recurrent pinguecula inflammation, allergic eye disease, and meibomian gland dysfunction maintain a low-grade inflammatory milieu that drives pterygium progression toward the visual axis.
HPV DNA is detected in roughly 20 to 25 percent of pterygium specimens in published series. Its causal role is debated, but co-infection may accelerate fibrovascular proliferation in some patients.

riskFactors.factor: Living within 37 degrees latitude of the equator
riskFactors.description: UV-B index is highest in this zone year-round. Prevalence in equatorial cohorts (Caribbean, sub-Saharan Africa, northern Australia, South Asia) reaches 15 to 25 percent.
More than five hours of daily outdoor exposure increases pterygium risk three to four times. Use of wraparound UV-blocking sunglasses and brimmed hats is protective.
Cumulative UV damage and decline in limbal stem-cell function with age explain rising prevalence; central encroachment is typically a fifth- or sixth-decade event.
Men are 2 to 3 times more affected, largely reflecting occupational outdoor exposure patterns rather than a true biological difference.
Recurrent pterygium after bare-sclera excision can be more aggressive and frequently re-encroaches centrally, particularly in patients younger than 40 and in fleshy (T3) morphology.

riskFactors.factor: Dry-eye disease and meibomian gland dysfunction
riskFactors.description: A disrupted tear film exposes the limbus to dehydration stress and inflammation, accelerating pterygium growth toward the cornea center.
Heritability is estimated at roughly 25 to 35 percent. Polymorphisms in TGF-beta pathway genes correlate with central encroachment.

riskFactors.factor: Welding, sun-lamp, and high-altitude UV exposure
riskFactors.description: Occupational arc-light and high-altitude UV are independent risks even outside the equatorial belt. Welders without proper eye protection have notably higher rates.

affectedDemographics: Global prevalence of pterygium is 10.2% (Liu 2013 meta-analysis of 21 population studies); central grade III-IV represents 5-15% of all pterygium cases
Highest rates in Indigenous Australian (20+%), Maori, Caribbean, and sub-Saharan African populations due to combined UV exposure and genetic susceptibility
Indian subcontinent: prevalence 9.5% in rural South India (Aravind Comprehensive Eye Survey); higher in coastal and agricultural populations
Men outnumber women by 2-3:1 in most series, reflecting outdoor occupational exposure
Rare in children; pediatric pterygium suggests xeroderma pigmentosum, congenital limbal stem-cell deficiency, or chronic ocular surface disease and warrants pediatric ophthalmology referral
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of central pterygium is clinical, made at the slit lamp by a cornea-trained ophthalmologist or experienced general ophthalmologist. The examiner measures the horizontal extent of the head of the pterygium from the limbus to the pupillary axis using slit-beam graticule, photographs the lesion under diffuse and slit illumination, and grades morphology by the Tan classification (T1 atrophic through T3 fleshy) based on visibility of underlying episcleral vessels. Corneal topography is mandatory before considering surgery in any pterygium within 3 mm of the visual axis: a Pentacam, Orbscan, or Placido-disc system quantifies the induced astigmatism magnitude and axis, identifies irregular astigmatism that explains reduced best-corrected acuity, and provides a baseline for postoperative visual rehabilitation. Induced astigmatism above 1.5 diopters is widely accepted as a strong surgical indication even in eyes without other symptoms, because the refractive component will not resolve with glasses alone. Adjunctive tests include specular microscopy of the central endothelium (to confirm endothelial reserve before considering future intraocular surgery), anterior segment OCT to map the depth of stromal invasion, and Schirmer or tear-break-up time to characterize the ocular surface before grafting. The critical differential is ocular surface squamous neoplasia (OSSN), which can masquerade as a fleshy pterygium in older or immunosuppressed patients; rapid growth, leukoplakia, or atypical morphology warrants excisional biopsy with frozen-section margin control. Pseudopterygium, a post-inflammatory adhesion of bulbar conjunctiva to peripheral cornea, is distinguished by the ability to pass a probe under the body of the lesion and the absence of true limbal involvement.

diagnosticTests.test: Slit-lamp biomicroscopy with pterygium measurement and Tan grading
diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines the size, vascularity, and exact distance from the head of the pterygium to the pupillary axis. Establishes the surgical indication and predicts recurrence risk based on T1-T3 morphology.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless 5-10 minute examination with eyes wide open under a bright microscope. The examiner may use fluorescein dye to highlight surface irregularity. Pupil dilation is not required for this step.

diagnosticTests.test: Corneal topography (Pentacam, Orbscan, or Placido system)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies pterygium-induced with-the-rule and irregular astigmatism, maps the meridian of corneal flattening, and provides preoperative and postoperative comparison for visual rehabilitation planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless scan that takes 1-2 minutes per eye. The patient looks at a fixation light while a series of light patterns are projected onto the cornea. No drops or dilation.

diagnosticTests.test: Best-corrected visual acuity and manifest refraction
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents the visual penalty caused by the pterygium and the irregular astigmatism component that cannot be corrected by glasses alone. Drives the timing of surgery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard letter chart at 6 meters with trial lenses. Takes 10-15 minutes including pinhole testing and astigmatic refinement.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Creates an objective record of pterygium morphology, vascularity, and extent over time, essential for medico-legal documentation, progression monitoring, and surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two to four high-resolution photographs in primary gaze and lateral gaze. Painless and brief; bright flash is required for adequate detail.

diagnosticTests.test: Specular microscopy of the corneal endothelium
diagnosticTests.purpose: Counts endothelial cell density before surgery, important because pterygium and ocular surface disease can mask early endothelial dysfunction that would influence anesthesia choice and future cataract surgery planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless 5-minute scan from a non-contact specular microscope. The patient fixates a light while the machine photographs the endothelial cell mosaic.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps the depth of stromal involvement, presence of pseudopterygium versus true pterygium, and any underlying ocular surface squamous neoplasia. Useful for surgical planning in atypical or recurrent cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-contact 1-minute scan per eye. No dye or dilation needed. Provides a cross-sectional image of the cornea and conjunctiva at the lesion.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive when ocular surface squamous neoplasia is suspected. Frozen-section margins guide intraoperative re-excision; permanent sections classify dysplasia versus invasion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under topical or peribulbar anesthesia as part of definitive pterygium removal. The specimen is sent to pathology and results return in 5-10 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of central pterygium is surgical. Once the lesion encroaches within 3 mm of the visual axis or induces more than 1.5 diopters of astigmatism, medical management with lubricants and topical steroids only buys time; it does not reverse the corneal distortion or remove the encroaching tissue. Surgical principles, established by Kenyon in 1985 and refined over four decades, are to excise the pterygium body and head cleanly down to bare sclera, polish residual corneal fibrovascular remnants with a diamond burr or blade, and immediately reconstruct the limbus with a free conjunctival autograft harvested from the superior bulbar conjunctiva of the same eye. The autograft is secured with 10-0 nylon sutures or, increasingly, with fibrin glue (Tisseel or Evicel), which shortens operative time and reduces postoperative discomfort without increasing recurrence. Adding a limbal stem-cell component (limbal-conjunctival autograft) further reduces recurrence to roughly 2 to 5 percent and is the preferred technique in young patients, T3 fleshy lesions, and recurrent disease. Intraoperative mitomycin C 0.02 percent applied for 2 to 3 minutes to the scleral bed is reserved for high-recurrence-risk eyes and reduces recurrence below 5 percent at the cost of rare but serious complications (scleral melt, delayed wound healing, secondary glaucoma). Amniotic membrane transplantation is used when defects are too large for autograft. The bare-sclera technique without grafting is abandoned: recurrence ranges from 30 to 90 percent in published series and is unacceptable for a central pterygium. Postoperative treatment consists of topical antibiotic for 1 week, topical steroid tapered over 4 to 6 weeks, and aggressive lubrication. Visual rehabilitation begins 6 to 12 weeks after surgery with repeat topography and refraction; residual irregular astigmatism may be addressed with rigid gas-permeable or scleral contact lenses, or in selected cases with topography-guided photorefractive keratectomy.

medicalTreatments.name: Preservative-free artificial tears and ocular lubricants
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for any symptomatic pterygium and used pre- and postoperatively to stabilize the tear film. Carboxymethylcellulose or hyaluronic acid drops 4-6 times daily reduce foreign body sensation, redness flares, and refractive fluctuation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom relief in 60-70% of pre-surgical patients; does not reduce pterygium size or recurrence.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical corticosteroids (loteprednol 0.5%, fluorometholone 0.1%, or prednisolone acetate 1%)
medicalTreatments.description: Used in short courses to control inflammatory flares of the pterygium body and as the standard postoperative anti-inflammatory taper over 4-6 weeks. Intraocular pressure must be monitored at 2 and 6 weeks because of steroid-response glaucoma risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Inflammation and redness reduce in 80-90% of treated flares within 1-2 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical cyclosporine 0.05% or lifitegrast 5%
medicalTreatments.description: Used as steroid-sparing maintenance for chronic ocular surface inflammation that aggravates pterygium symptoms. Twice-daily dosing for 12+ weeks. Not a substitute for surgery in central encroachment.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves dry-eye signs in 50-60% of treated patients; no measurable effect on pterygium size.

medicalTreatments.name: Intraoperative mitomycin C 0.02% (high-risk eyes)
medicalTreatments.description: Applied for 2-3 minutes to the scleral bed during pterygium excision in patients at high recurrence risk (young, fleshy T3, recurrent disease). Reduces recurrence to roughly 3% in pooled data.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces recurrence by 60-80% versus no antimetabolite; balanced against rare scleral melt and delayed epithelial healing.

medicalTreatments.name: Postoperative topical antibiotic (moxifloxacin, ofloxacin, or chloramphenicol)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard 1-week course after surgery to prevent infection of the autograft and ocular surface. Drops are typically used four times daily until the surface re-epithelializes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Postoperative infection rate is below 1% with appropriate prophylaxis.

surgicalOptions.name: Conjunctival autograft pterygium excision (Kenyon technique)
surgicalOptions.description: Gold-standard procedure. The pterygium is dissected from the cornea and sclera, residual corneal tissue is polished smooth with a diamond burr, and a free conjunctival graft from the superior bulbar conjunctiva of the same eye is sutured or glued onto the bare scleral bed. Performed under topical or peribulbar anesthesia in 30-45 minutes.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence rate 5-10% in published series (Hirst 2008 systematic review); visual acuity improves in over 80% of patients with central encroachment.

surgicalOptions.name: Limbal-conjunctival autograft
surgicalOptions.description: Modification that includes a 0.5-1 mm strip of donor-eye limbus with the conjunctival graft, restoring the limbal stem-cell barrier directly. Preferred in young patients, fleshy T3 lesions, and recurrent pterygium.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence 2-5% in randomized trials versus 10-15% for plain conjunctival autograft.

surgicalOptions.name: Pterygium excision with fibrin glue fixation
surgicalOptions.description: Identical to autograft excision but the graft is fixed with fibrin sealant (Tisseel, Evicel) rather than 10-0 nylon sutures. Shorter operative time (typically 10-15 minutes saved), less postoperative inflammation, and faster patient recovery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Equivalent or slightly lower recurrence than sutures in meta-analyses; postoperative pain scores significantly lower at 24 hours.

surgicalOptions.name: Amniotic membrane transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Used when the conjunctival defect is too large for autograft (double-headed pterygium, recurrent disease with depleted donor conjunctiva). Cryopreserved or dehydrated amniotic membrane is sutured or glued to the bare sclera.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence 10-25% as a primary procedure; better as adjunct to autograft. Useful in eyes where future glaucoma filtering surgery may require preserved conjunctiva.

surgicalOptions.name: Bare-sclera excision (historical, not recommended for central pterygium)
surgicalOptions.description: Original technique in which the pterygium is removed and the sclera is left to re-epithelialize spontaneously. Now considered substandard for central pterygium because of high recurrence.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence 30-90% in published series; obsolete in modern practice for visually significant lesions.

alternativeTreatments.name: Sun protection with wraparound UV-blocking sunglasses
alternativeTreatments.description: Slows pterygium growth and reduces postoperative recurrence. UV-400 wraparound lenses with side coverage are recommended for all patients with pterygium, particularly outdoor workers, and lifelong after surgery.

alternativeTreatments.name: Workplace ergonomics and humidification
alternativeTreatments.description: For patients in dry, windy, or dusty environments, eye protection (safety goggles), workplace humidification, and frequent lubricant drops reduce ocular surface inflammation that drives pterygium progression.

whySeeSpecialist: Central pterygium requires a cornea-trained ophthalmologist for two reasons. First, the surgical decision and technique selection (autograft technique, antimetabolite use, fibrin glue versus sutures, amniotic membrane requirement) depend on judgment of recurrence risk, morphology, and ocular surface health that comes with cornea fellowship training. Second, visual rehabilitation after central pterygium surgery is rarely complete with glasses alone, and managing residual irregular astigmatism with specialty contact lenses or topography-guided refractive surgery is itself a subspecialty domain. A general ophthalmologist may manage peripheral pterygium and uncomplicated lesions, but a cornea specialist should evaluate any pterygium within 3 mm of the visual axis, any lesion exceeding 1.5 diopters of induced astigmatism, all recurrent pterygia, and any suspicious atypical lesion that could be ocular surface squamous neoplasia.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for fellowship training in cornea and external disease (the typical post-residency 1- to 2-year subspecialty), regular volume of pterygium surgery (at least 20-30 cases per year), experience with limbal-conjunctival autograft and fibrin glue techniques, comfort with intraoperative mitomycin C in selected cases, and access to corneal topography and anterior segment OCT in the clinic. Ask about the surgeon's personal recurrence rate (a competent cornea specialist should quote a number below 10 percent for primary central pterygium) and the planned approach to postoperative visual rehabilitation. Continuity matters because the postoperative course extends to 6-12 weeks for topography review and possibly months for refractive stabilization.
preventionStrategies: Wear wraparound UV-400 sunglasses outdoors year-round, especially within 2 hours of solar noon and at high altitude or near reflective surfaces (water, sand, snow) — UV is the single strongest pterygium risk factor
Use a wide-brimmed hat in addition to sunglasses; this reduces ocular UV exposure by a further 30-50 percent
Use protective goggles in dusty, windy, or chemically irritating workplaces such as construction, welding, and agriculture
Treat dry eye disease and meibomian gland dysfunction actively — a stable tear film slows pterygium progression even when other risk factors are unchanged
Lifelong UV protection after pterygium surgery; recurrence is more common in unprotected eyes, and the second pterygium is typically harder to remove
lifestyleModifications: Schedule outdoor work outside peak UV hours (10 am to 4 pm) when possible, especially in tropical and equatorial latitudes
Replace lost sunglasses or scratched UV-blocking lenses promptly; scratched coatings reduce protection
Use lubricant drops 4-6 times daily during dry, dusty, or air-conditioned conditions to support the tear film
Avoid rubbing the eyes — mechanical irritation accelerates pterygium growth and aggravates postoperative graft healing
Stop smoking; tobacco smoke is independently associated with ocular surface inflammation and pterygium progression
Maintain control of any contributing systemic conditions such as rosacea or atopic disease that worsen ocular surface inflammation

recommended: Diet rich in carotenoids (carrots, sweet potatoes, leafy greens) and vitamin A — supports ocular surface epithelial health
Omega-3 fatty acids (fatty fish, walnuts, flaxseed) — improve meibomian gland function and tear film stability
Adequate hydration of 2 liters daily — supports tear production and ocular surface health
Vitamin C and E from fresh fruit and vegetables — antioxidant support against UV-induced oxidative stress
Zinc-containing foods such as legumes, nuts, and shellfish — cofactor for corneal epithelial repair
avoid: Chronic dehydration from inadequate fluid intake, which thins the tear film and worsens surface symptoms
Excess alcohol intake, which dehydrates and disrupts the lipid layer of the tear film
Ultra-processed diets low in antioxidants and omega-3 — associated with worse dry eye signs in cohort studies
Smoking and secondhand smoke exposure, which increase oxidative damage to the conjunctiva and cornea

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise is unrestricted before and between pterygium care. After pterygium surgery, avoid swimming and contact sports for 2 weeks and high-intensity activity that involves bending or straining for 1 week to protect the autograft. Always wear UV-blocking wraparound sunglasses outdoors during exercise, particularly cycling, running, and water sports, which combine UV exposure with wind and surface reflection.
dailyManagement: Apply preservative-free lubricant drops 4-6 times daily for symptom control and tear film support
Wear UV-400 wraparound sunglasses every time outdoors, including overcast days
Use a 5-minute warm compress to the lids daily if associated meibomian gland dysfunction is present
Photograph the eye every 3-6 months in the same lighting to track pterygium progression
Attend ophthalmology review every 6-12 months while the pterygium is observed, and immediately if vision changes
prognosis: With timely surgical removal by conjunctival autograft, the prognosis for central pterygium is good. Recurrence rates of 5 to 10 percent are achievable in primary surgery, and recurrence rates fall below 5 percent with limbal-conjunctival autograft or adjunctive mitomycin C in high-risk eyes. Most patients regain best-corrected acuity within one to two lines of their preoperative potential by 12 weeks after surgery, and induced astigmatism reduces by 1 to 4 diopters in eyes where the lesion was advanced. Residual irregular astigmatism in 10 to 20 percent of patients may require rigid gas-permeable or scleral contact lenses, or in selected cases topography-guided photorefractive keratectomy 6 to 12 months after the index surgery. Without treatment, central pterygium continues to advance, causing progressive corneal scarring, irregular astigmatism above 3 diopters, and amblyopia in long-standing untreated adults. The strongest prognostic factors are pterygium morphology (atrophic T1 best, fleshy T3 highest recurrence), surgical technique (autograft beats bare sclera by 6 to 10 fold), and consistent lifelong UV protection after surgery.
recoveryTimeline: Conjunctival autograft surgery is performed as a day case under topical or peribulbar anesthesia and takes 30-45 minutes. The eye is patched overnight; vision is blurred and the eye is red and uncomfortable for 3-5 days. Most patients return to office work within a week and to outdoor work after 2 weeks. The autograft becomes seamlessly integrated by 4-6 weeks, and topical steroids are tapered off over 4-6 weeks. Refractive stabilization on topography takes 6-12 weeks, at which point updated glasses, contact lens fitting, or further refractive surgery can be planned. Most recurrences, if they occur, manifest within the first 3-12 months postoperatively; the risk falls steeply after one year.
complications: Pterygium recurrence (5-10% with autograft, up to 30-90% with bare sclera) — often more aggressive and harder to remove than the primary lesion
Residual irregular astigmatism after surgery, requiring rigid gas-permeable or scleral contact lenses for full visual rehabilitation in 10-20% of cases
Permanent corneal stromal scarring under the original pterygium head, which can persist even after successful removal and limit best-corrected acuity
Mitomycin C-related scleral melt or delayed epithelial healing — rare but serious complication of antimetabolite use
Conjunctival graft retraction, dehiscence, or pyogenic granuloma formation, requiring revision in roughly 2-5% of cases
Diplopia from extraocular muscle restriction in eyes with extensive surgery or recurrent pterygium involving the medial rectus insertion
Misdiagnosed ocular surface squamous neoplasia masquerading as pterygium, with delayed cancer diagnosis if biopsy is not performed in atypical lesions

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — Pterygium Information
supportResources.description: Patient-facing pages from the AAO covering diagnosis, surgery options, and recovery.

supportResources.name: AAO Preferred Practice Pattern — Cornea and External Disease
supportResources.description: Clinician-facing guidelines that include pterygium management recommendations.

supportResources.name: EyeWiki — Pterygium
supportResources.description: Peer-reviewed wiki maintained by the AAO with detailed surgical technique descriptions and recurrence data.

supportResources.name: National Eye Institute — Eye Health Information
supportResources.description: US National Institutes of Health patient-facing eye health portal.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peripheral Pterygium
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peripheral pterygium has not crossed onto the central cornea and does not affect vision or induce significant astigmatism. It is managed by observation, lubricants, and UV protection, not surgery. Central pterygium has crossed onto the visual axis area, induces measurable astigmatism, and is a surgical lesion.

confusedWithConditions.name: Recurrent Pterygium
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Recurrent pterygium is fibrovascular regrowth after prior surgery, typically more vascular and aggressive, with higher re-recurrence rates. Central pterygium denotes the lesion's anatomic encroachment on the visual axis regardless of whether it is primary or recurrent.

confusedWithConditions.name: Pseudopterygium
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pseudopterygium is a conjunctival adhesion to peripheral cornea following chemical, thermal, or inflammatory injury — a probe can be passed underneath the body of the lesion, and there is no true limbal stem-cell involvement. True central pterygium is firmly adherent to the cornea and originates from the limbus.

confusedWithConditions.name: Age-Related Cataract
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cataract causes painless gradual visual blur from lens opacity, with no visible surface lesion. Central pterygium causes blur from corneal astigmatism and shows a visible pink fibrovascular growth on the cornea. The two can coexist in older patients and need separate management.

relatedConditions.name: Pterygium (Umbrella)
relatedConditions.relevance: Central pterygium is the advanced anatomic stage of the same disease entity. Patients with peripheral pterygium need monitoring for central encroachment.

relatedConditions.name: Recurrent Pterygium
relatedConditions.relevance: Sibling condition — central recurrence is the most common cause of repeat surgery and is managed with limbal-conjunctival autograft plus mitomycin C.

relatedConditions.name: Corneal Disorders (Umbrella)
relatedConditions.relevance: Central pterygium falls under the broader category of corneal surface disorders and shares risk factors with limbal stem-cell deficiency.

relatedConditions.name: Age-Related Cataract
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently coexists in older outdoor workers; pterygium must be removed first to stabilize the corneal curvature before reliable cataract IOL power calculation.

relatedConditions.name: Primary Open-Angle Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Both share an older outdoor demographic; postoperative pterygium steroid use can transiently raise intraocular pressure in glaucoma patients and warrants monitoring.

faqs.question: What is central pterygium of the eye?
faqs.answer: Central pterygium is a pink fibrovascular growth that has crossed onto the cornea and reached the central vision zone, distorting corneal curvature. It causes blurred vision from induced astigmatism, foreign body sensation, and redness. It is the advanced stage of pterygium and requires surgery.

faqs.question: Why is central pterygium more serious than peripheral pterygium?
faqs.answer: Central pterygium encroaches on the visual axis and induces 1 to 7 diopters of astigmatism that glasses cannot fully correct. Peripheral pterygium stays at the corner of the cornea, does not affect vision, and only needs observation. Untreated central encroachment causes permanent scarring.

faqs.question: Do I need surgery for central pterygium?
faqs.answer: Yes. Once a pterygium reaches within 3 mm of the pupillary axis or induces more than 1.5 diopters of astigmatism, surgical removal is recommended. Lubricants and steroids only ease symptoms; they do not reverse corneal distortion or shrink the lesion.

faqs.question: What is the best surgery for central pterygium?
faqs.answer: Conjunctival autograft excision is the gold standard. The pterygium is removed and a graft from the patient's own superior conjunctiva is sutured or glued onto the bare sclera. Recurrence is 5 to 10 percent, far better than 30 to 90 percent with older bare-sclera excision.

faqs.question: What is mitomycin C and when is it used in pterygium surgery?
faqs.answer: Mitomycin C 0.02 percent is a chemotherapy agent applied to the scleral bed for 2 to 3 minutes to reduce recurrence. It is used in high-risk cases such as recurrent pterygium and fleshy T3 lesions, lowering recurrence to roughly 3 percent. Rare side effects include scleral melt and delayed healing.

faqs.question: How much astigmatism does a central pterygium cause?
faqs.answer: Central pterygium typically induces 1 to 7 diopters of with-the-rule astigmatism, depending on how close the head is to the pupillary axis. The astigmatism rises steeply over the final 1 to 2 millimeters of central encroachment. Topography is the most accurate way to measure it before surgery.

faqs.question: Will surgery restore my vision after central pterygium?
faqs.answer: Usually yes. Visual acuity improves by one or more lines in over 80 percent of patients within 12 weeks as the cornea flattens back. Residual irregular astigmatism remains in 10 to 20 percent of patients and can be managed with rigid contact lenses or topography-guided refractive surgery.

faqs.question: How long is recovery after pterygium surgery?
faqs.answer: Most patients return to office work within a week and to outdoor work within 2 weeks. The autograft integrates by 4 to 6 weeks. Topical steroid drops are tapered over 4 to 6 weeks, and refractive stabilization is reached at 6 to 12 weeks, when updated glasses or contact lenses can be prescribed.

faqs.question: Can central pterygium come back after surgery?
faqs.answer: Recurrence occurs in 5 to 10 percent of patients after conjunctival autograft and below 5 percent with limbal-conjunctival autograft or mitomycin C. Most recurrences happen in the first 3 to 12 months. Lifelong UV protection with wraparound sunglasses substantially reduces the risk of recurrence.

faqs.question: What causes central pterygium?
faqs.answer: Chronic ultraviolet light exposure is the strongest cause, which is why pterygium is common near the equator and in outdoor workers. Other contributors include dust and wind exposure, dry eye, genetic susceptibility, and chronic ocular surface inflammation.

faqs.question: Is pterygium the same as a cataract?
faqs.answer: No. Pterygium is a fibrovascular growth on the eye surface that crosses onto the cornea, while cataract is clouding of the lens inside the eye. Both cause blurred vision and both are more common in older outdoor workers, but they are different diseases treated by different procedures.

faqs.question: What is the Tan grading of pterygium?
faqs.answer: Tan grading classifies pterygium by how clearly the underlying episcleral vessels are seen. T1 atrophic shows clear vessels and has the lowest recurrence. T2 intermediate has partly visible vessels. T3 fleshy obscures vessels and has the highest recurrence, sometimes above 20 percent.

faqs.question: Can I prevent pterygium getting bigger without surgery?
faqs.answer: Wraparound UV-400 sunglasses, brimmed hats, and goggles in dusty workplaces slow pterygium progression but do not reverse it. Lubricant drops control symptoms but not size. Once a pterygium reaches the visual axis, only surgery restores corneal curvature and vision.

faqs.question: Are there any side effects of pterygium surgery?
faqs.answer: Common short-term effects include redness, foreign body sensation, and tearing for 1 to 2 weeks. Rare complications include graft retraction (2 to 5 percent), recurrence, pyogenic granuloma, transient diplopia, and steroid pressure rise. Scleral melt is rare and largely linked to mitomycin C.

faqs.question: Is pterygium surgery painful?
faqs.answer: The procedure itself is painless under topical or peribulbar anesthesia. The first 24 to 72 hours involve moderate gritty discomfort, well controlled with oral analgesics and topical lubricants. Fibrin glue fixation rather than sutures reduces postoperative pain significantly.

faqs.question: Can pterygium become cancer?
faqs.answer: Pterygium itself is benign. However, ocular surface squamous neoplasia can sometimes resemble a fleshy pterygium, especially in older or immunosuppressed patients. Atypical, rapidly growing, or keratinized lesions should be biopsied during excision to exclude malignancy.

faqs.question: Can I drive after central pterygium surgery?
faqs.answer: Most surgeons advise no driving for 24 to 48 hours because of patching, blurred vision, and anesthetic effects. Once the eye is comfortable and vision adequate, usually within 3 to 7 days, driving can resume. Final acuity for prescription updates is reached at 6 to 12 weeks.

faqs.question: Does central pterygium affect both eyes?
faqs.answer: Pterygium can develop in either eye and bilateral involvement is common in patients with high cumulative UV exposure. Each eye is staged and treated independently. If one eye has central pterygium, the second eye should be examined and protected aggressively.

faqs.question: How much does central pterygium surgery cost?
faqs.answer: Costs vary by country and technique. In India, conjunctival autograft surgery typically costs 15,000 to 40,000 rupees per eye. In the United States, it ranges from 1,500 to 3,500 dollars per eye. Most insurance plans cover surgery when it is medically indicated.

faqs.question: Will I need glasses or contact lenses after surgery?
faqs.answer: Usually yes. Some residual astigmatism remains after pterygium removal and is corrected with glasses. About 10 to 20 percent of patients have enough irregular astigmatism to benefit from rigid contact lenses or topography-guided photorefractive keratectomy 6 to 12 months after surgery.

simpleName: Central pterygium (pterygium reaching the center of the cornea)
symptomKeywords: pink growth on eye crossing pupil
metaTitle: Central Pterygium: Symptoms, Surgery & Recovery | aihealz
metaDescription: Central pterygium crosses the cornea and blurs vision through induced astigmatism. Surgery, recurrence rates, and recovery reviewed by our medical board.

images.altText: Clinical photograph of an eye showing a pterygium extending from the inner corner onto the cornea toward the pupil
images.caption: Pterygium extending onto the cornea — when it reaches the pupillary axis it is classified as central pterygium.

images.altText: Slit-lamp close-up of a fleshy pterygium with prominent vascularity crossing the limbus
images.caption: Slit-lamp view of a fleshy (Tan T3) pterygium with prominent vascularity — a high-recurrence morphology.
